topic: AC wars at the clinic — patients freezing during treatment

turns.content: can I vent about something that drives me absolutely INSANE at work??
Absolutely, vent away. What's going on?

THE AIR CONDITIONING. Gente. Our clinic keeps the AC at like 18 degrees and my patients are literally shivering on the treatment table. I had a guy today trying to do shoulder mobilizations and his muscles were so cold and tense that I couldn't even get proper range of motion! How am I supposed to do soft tissue work on a muscle that's basically frozen??

That's a real clinical issue, not just a comfort complaint. Cold muscles have increased tone and reduced elasticity, which makes manual therapy less effective and can even increase the risk of strain during mobilization. Is the thermostat centrally controlled, or do you have any ability to adjust the temperature in your treatment rooms?

centrally controlled! That's the whole problem! The admin office controls it for the entire building and they keep it arctic because THEY like it cold. But they're sitting at desks in sweaters! My patients are lying on a table in shorts and a t-shirt getting hands-on treatment! It's completely different, you can't have the same temperature for an office and a treatment room, ai meu Deus

You're absolutely right — the thermal needs of a rehab clinic and an administrative office are fundamentally different. For manual therapy and exercise-based rehab, the ideal room temperature is generally around 22-24 degrees Celsius. Patients in minimal clothing on a treatment table lose heat quickly, and that cold directly works against what you're trying to achieve therapeutically. Have you raised this formally with management?

oh I've raised it MULTIPLE times haha. I've sent emails, I've talked to the operations manager, I even printed out a research article about temperature and muscle tissue response. They nodded very politely and nothing changed. So now I have a collection of blankets in my treatment room like I'm running a bed and breakfast, not a sports rehab clinic

The blanket collection is a practical workaround, but it shouldn't be necessary. A couple other options: hot packs applied before manual therapy can pre-warm the tissue you're working on, and a small portable space heater — if allowed — could take the edge off. For the bigger fight, if you can get other therapists to co-sign a formal request, collective pressure is harder to dismiss than individual complaints.

oh believe me the other PTs feel the same way! We're all suffering! One of my colleagues started calling our hallway the 'frozen corridor' haha. And yes I do use hot packs before treatment but that adds time to every session, and we're already pushed on time as it is. It's so frustrating because the fix is SO simple — just turn the AC up 4 degrees!

The time cost of pre-warming is actually a strong argument. If you can quantify it — like "we're spending an extra 5-7 minutes per patient on hot packs that wouldn't be necessary at an appropriate temperature" — that translates directly into lost appointment slots and revenue. Management tends to respond to numbers better than comfort complaints.

oh that's SMART. I hadn't thought about framing it as lost revenue. That might actually work because gente, these people care about numbers, they love their KPIs. If I show them we're losing treatment time because the building is a freezer, maybe they'll actually listen

Exactly. If each therapist loses even one appointment slot per day because of pre-warming time, multiply that by the number of therapists, by the days in a month, by the per-session revenue. That number will be significant. A one-page summary with those dollar figures will probably accomplish more than a hundred emails about being cold.

okay I'm genuinely going to do this. I'll track it for a week — how many patients need pre-warming, how long it takes each time — and then present the data. Nossa, data-driven thermostat negotiation. Never thought I'd be doing that in my career haha. But honestly I also worry about patient satisfaction. Some of my patients have actually mentioned the cold in their feedback forms!

That's a goldmine — patient feedback mentioning the cold is hard for management to dismiss. If you can pull those specific comments alongside your time-cost data, you're building a case from two angles: revenue impact and patient experience. Both of those are metrics that clinic leadership typically watches closely. The combination should be much more persuasive than either one alone.

you're right! I know at least three patients who wrote something about it. I'll ask the front desk if they can pull those comments for me. This is turning into a full presentation haha. My colleagues are going to love this — I'll be the hero who finally got the temperature changed. If it works. WHEN it works. Positive thinking!

That's the spirit. Revenue data plus patient complaints plus a collective request from the therapy team — that's a strong trifecta. Even the most KPI-focused manager would have a hard time saying no to that. Keep me posted on the thermostat victory!

haha I will! 22 degrees, that's all I ask!! My patients deserve warm muscles and my hands deserve to not feel like ice cubes. Thank you querida, this was so helpful. Time to go collect my data — Bruna the PT slash thermostat activist haha. Meu bem, you're the best!
Bruna the Thermostat Activist has a nice ring to it! Your patients are lucky to have a PT who fights for the right treatment conditions. Go get those 4 degrees back — I'm rooting for 22!
